one_liner: A 32-year-old African American woman presents with 2 months of dry cough, progressive dyspnea on exertion, fatigue, and painful red nodules on her shins.

stage2.hpi_extension: She also reports intermittent eye redness with blurred vision, unexplained fevers, and mild joint pain affecting her ankles. She has had no sick contacts, no travel history, and works as an accountant. She denies hemoptysis, night sweats, or weight loss of significance. Her chest symptoms began insidiously and have been slowly worsening.
stage2.social_history: Non-smoker, rare alcohol, no illicit drugs. No occupational exposures. Born in the US.
stage2.physical_exam: General: well-appearing woman in no distress. HEENT: **anterior uveitis with conjunctival injection** on slit lamp; parotid gland mildly enlarged bilaterally. Neck: no cervical lymphadenopathy. Pulmonary: fine end-inspiratory crackles at the bases, no wheezes. Cardiac: regular, no murmurs. Skin: **tender erythematous nodules on bilateral anterior shins (erythema nodosum)**; violaceous plaques on the nasal bridge and cheeks (**lupus pernio**). MSK: ankle arthralgias with mild swelling.

lab_results.result: Chest X-ray: **bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy** with reticulonodular infiltrates in the upper lung fields (**Stage II sarcoidosis**). High-resolution CT: **eggshell calcifications** of mediastinal lymph nodes and perilymphatic micronodules along bronchovascular bundles and fissures.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with eggshell calcifications is the classic sarcoidosis imaging finding. Staging: I = BHL alone; II = BHL + infiltrates; III = infiltrates without BHL; IV = fibrosis.

lab_results.result: Transbronchial lung biopsy: **non-caseating granulomas** with epithelioid histiocytes, multinucleated giant cells, and scattered **Schaumann bodies (calcified laminated concretions)** and **asteroid bodies (stellate inclusions)**. AFB and fungal stains negative. Culture negative.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Non-caseating granulomas are the histologic hallmark of sarcoidosis. Diagnosis requires exclusion of TB and fungal infections, which cause caseating (necrotizing) granulomas.

lab_results.result: **Hypercalcemia (calcium 11.4 mg/dL)**, **elevated serum ACE level**, mild transaminitis. 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D elevated. 24-hour urine calcium elevated.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Hypercalcemia in sarcoidosis is due to 1α-hydroxylase activity in activated macrophages within granulomas, converting 25-OH vitamin D to 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D. Elevated ACE (from granulomas) is supportive but neither sensitive nor specific.

stage5.final_clue: She is started on oral corticosteroids with improvement of her pulmonary symptoms, skin lesions, and uveitis; her hypercalcemia resolves and she is counseled to avoid sun exposure and vitamin D supplementation.

debrief.classic_presentation: Sarcoidosis classically presents in young adults (20–40 years), more common in African Americans and women, with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, dry cough, and dyspnea. Extrapulmonary manifestations include erythema nodosum, lupus pernio (violaceous facial plaques), anterior uveitis, parotid enlargement, hypercalcemia, arthritis, cardiac involvement (conduction blocks), and cranial nerve palsies (especially CN VII — Heerfordt syndrome = uveitis + parotitis + fever + facial palsy). Löfgren syndrome = erythema nodosum + BHL + arthritis + fever.
debrief.pathophysiology: Sarcoidosis is a **systemic non-caseating granulomatous disease** of unknown etiology, characterized by exaggerated Th1 response with increased IFN-γ, IL-2, and TNF-α, leading to granuloma formation. Granulomas consist of activated macrophages (epithelioid histiocytes), CD4+ T cells, and multinucleated giant cells. Classic histologic features include **Schaumann bodies** (laminated concretions of calcium and protein) and **asteroid bodies** (stellate inclusions in giant cells). Hypercalcemia results from macrophage 1α-hydroxylase activity producing excess active vitamin D.
debrief.treatment_overview: Many patients (especially those with Löfgren syndrome) have spontaneous remission and require only observation. Symptomatic disease: systemic corticosteroids (prednisone) are first-line. Steroid-sparing agents for refractory disease: methotrexate, azathioprine, hydroxychloroquine (especially for cutaneous), TNF-α inhibitors (infliximab). Avoid vitamin D supplementation and sun exposure (they worsen hypercalcemia). Screen for cardiac involvement with ECG and cardiac MRI.
high_yield_pearls: Classic demographic: young African American woman with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and non-caseating granulomas.
Hypercalcemia in sarcoidosis is from macrophage 1α-hydroxylase producing active vitamin D (1,25-OH2-D) within granulomas.
Löfgren syndrome = erythema nodosum + bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy + polyarthralgia + fever (excellent prognosis, often self-resolves).
Schaumann bodies (laminated calcium/protein concretions) and asteroid bodies (stellate giant cell inclusions) are characteristic histologic findings.
Heerfordt syndrome (uveoparotid fever) = anterior uveitis + parotid enlargement + facial nerve palsy + fever.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Respiratory chapter, 'Restrictive lung diseases — Sarcoidosis' section.
